questions.to_find: The output printed by the program when count(3) is called

understand.plain: The function count(n) uses a static variable d that retains its value across recursive calls. Each call prints the current n, then the current d, increments d, recurses if n > 1, and finally prints d again after returning. Because d is static, all recursive calls share the same d, so by the time the recursion unwinds, d has its final value for all the trailing printf statements.

keywords.explain: A variable declared with 'static' inside a function retains its value between function calls (not re-initialized each call).
keywords.example: static int d = 1; — d is initialized only once; subsequent calls use the last stored value.

keywords.explain: After a recursive call returns, execution continues at the statement following the call. The printf after the if executes on the way back up.
keywords.example: count(3) calls count(2) which calls count(1). After count(1) returns, count(2) executes its trailing printf, then count(3) executes its trailing printf.

understand.visual_alt: Call stack trace showing count(3) calling count(2) calling count(1). Forward phase prints: 3,1 then 2,2 then 1,3. After d becomes 4, the unwinding phase prints: 4, 4, 4.

given.aim: Determine the exact output sequence printed by count(3) considering static variable behavior and recursion unwinding

terms.meaning: d is initialized once to 1 and shared across all recursive invocations of count
terms.example: After count(3) increments d to 2, count(2) sees d=2, not d=1
terms.connects: d is incremented by each call before recursing, so after all 3 calls d=4; all trailing printfs print 4

terms.term: printf("%d", n) and printf("%d", d) before recursion
terms.meaning: On the way down, each call prints its own n and the current d (before incrementing)
terms.example: count(3) prints 3 then 1; count(2) prints 2 then 2; count(1) prints 1 then 3
terms.connects: Produces the prefix '312213' in the output

terms.meaning: On the way back up (recursion unwinding), each call prints d; by then d=4 for all three
terms.example: count(1) prints 4, then count(2) prints 4, then count(3) prints 4
terms.connects: Produces the suffix '444' in the output

given.plan: Simulate the execution: trace the forward (downward) recursion printing n and d before increment, then track d incrementing through calls. On the way back up, all three trailing printfs print the final value of d=4.

steps.title: Forward phase — printing on the way down
steps.apply:
count(3): prints n=3, d=1, then d becomes 2.
count(2): prints n=2, d=2, then d becomes 3.
count(1): prints n=1, d=3, then d becomes 4.
(n=1 so no further recursion.)
Forward output so far: 3 1 2 2 1 3
steps.note: Each call sees the incremented value of d from the previous call because d is static.

steps.title: Backward phase — printing on the way back up
steps.apply:
count(1) executes its trailing printf: d=4, prints 4.
count(2) resumes after recursive call, executes trailing printf: d=4, prints 4.
count(3) resumes after recursive call, executes trailing printf: d=4, prints 4.
Backward output: 4 4 4
steps.note: d is static, so all three trailing printfs see the same final value d=4 (set by count(1)).

steps.title: Combine full output
steps.apply: Complete output = forward phase + backward phase = 312213 + 444 = 312213444

questions.formula_note: Static variable scope in C: a static local variable is initialized only once and retains its value across all calls to the function, including recursive calls.

questions.to_find: The value printed by the program when f(a, 5) is called on array {3, 5, 2, 6, 4}

understand.plain: The function f receives a pointer to the start of a subarray and the subarray length n. It returns the maximum value of (current element minus next element) across all consecutive pairs in the array. Each recursive call moves one step forward in the array and computes p[0]-p[1] for that position.

keywords.explain: p+1 advances the pointer to the next array element, so each recursive call works on the subarray starting one element later.
keywords.example: If p points to a[1]=5, then p+1 points to a[2]=2.

keywords.explain: At each level, p[0]-p[1] computes the difference of the current pair, and max() picks the largest such difference seen across all pairs.
keywords.example: For pairs (3,5),(5,2),(2,6),(6,4): differences are -2, 3, -4, 2; the max is 3.

understand.visual_alt: Array {3,5,2,6,4} with consecutive differences: 3-5=-2, 5-2=3, 2-6=-4, 6-4=2. The maximum difference is 3 (highlighted at pair 5,2).

given.aim: Find the maximum value of (p[i] - p[i+1]) over all consecutive pairs in the array

terms.meaning: When n <= 1 (subarray has one or fewer elements), return 0 — no pair to compare
terms.connects: Stops the recursion when the subarray is too short to form a pair

terms.meaning: The difference between the first and second element of the current subarray pointer
terms.connects: One candidate for the maximum; compared against the result of the recursive call

terms.meaning: The function returns the larger of the current pair's difference and the best difference found in the rest of the array
terms.connects: Ensures the global maximum consecutive difference is propagated up the call stack

given.plan: Expand the recursion bottom-up: start from the innermost call f({4},1)=0, compute each level's max(recursive_result, pair_difference), working back to f(a,5).

steps.title: Base and innermost recursive cases
steps.note: Negative differences are valid candidates but lose to non-negative ones.

steps.title: Outer recursive cases
steps.note: The maximum consecutive difference in the entire array is 3 (between elements 5 and 2 at indices 1 and 2).

solution.result: The value printed is 3 — option A.

questions.formula_note: The function computes max(p[i] - p[i+1]) over all i via recursion — equivalent to scanning all consecutive pairs and returning the largest difference.

questions.to_find: The output printed when fun1(5) is called with the two mutually recursive functions fun1 and fun2

understand.plain: fun1 and fun2 are mutually recursive functions. fun1(n) prints n, calls fun2(n-2), then prints n again. fun2(n) prints n, calls fun1(n-1), then prints n again. The recursion terminates when n reaches 0. Each function prints its n both before and after the recursive call, creating a palindrome-like output pattern.

keywords.explain: Two functions that call each other: fun1 calls fun2, and fun2 calls fun1.
keywords.example: fun1(5) calls fun2(3), which calls fun1(2), which calls fun2(0) — chain stops at 0.

keywords.explain: Each function prints n once before the recursive call and once after. This means each value is printed on both the way in and the way out.
keywords.example: fun1(5): prints 5 going in, calls fun2(3), then prints 5 coming out.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe y='96' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>→ fun2(3)</text><text x='70' y='258' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>print 5</text><rect x='145' y='60' width='120' height='200' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='80' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>fun2(3)</text><text x='205' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>print 3</text><text x='205' y='118' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>→ fun1(2)</text><text x='205' y='238' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>print 3</text><rect x='280' y='82' width='120' height='160' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='102' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>fun1(2)</text><text x='340' y='122' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>print 2</text><text x='340' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>→ fun2(0)</text><text x='340' y='218' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>print 2</text><rect x='415' y='102' width='100' height='80' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='465' y='122' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>fun2(0)</text><text x='465' y='148' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>n==0</text><text x='465' y='166' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>return</text><text x='260' y='290' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Output: 5 3 2 (return) 2 3 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Mutual recursion call chain: fun1(5) calls fun2(3) calls fun1(2) calls fun2(0). fun2(0) returns immediately (n==0). Unwinding prints in reverse: 2, 3, 5. Combined with forward prints: 5, 3, 2, 2, 3, 5.

given.aim: Trace the execution of mutually recursive fun1(5) to determine the printed output sequence

terms.meaning: fun1 calls fun2(n-2), so the argument drops by 2 at each fun1->fun2 transition
terms.example: fun1(5) calls fun2(3); fun1(2) calls fun2(0)
terms.connects: The chain fun1(5)->fun2(3)->fun1(2)->fun2(0) terminates when fun2 gets n=0

terms.meaning: fun2 calls fun1(n-1), so the argument drops by 1 at each fun2->fun1 transition
terms.connects: Controls how deep the mutual recursion goes before reaching 0

terms.meaning: Each call prints n before calling its partner and again after it returns, creating a palindrome around the base
terms.connects: The full sequence mirrors itself: first half is 5,3,2 and second half is 2,3,5

given.plan: Trace the call chain forward noting each print, find the base case, then trace the return path noting each trailing print.

steps.title: Forward trace — prints on the way down
steps.apply:
fun1(5): print 5, call fun2(3)
fun2(3): print 3, call fun1(2)
fun1(2): print 2, call fun2(0)
fun2(0): n==0, return immediately (no print)
Forward output: 5 3 2
steps.note: fun2(0) hits the base case and returns without printing anything.

steps.title: Backward trace — prints on the way back up
steps.apply:
fun1(2) resumes after fun2(0) returns: print 2
fun2(3) resumes after fun1(2) returns: print 3
fun1(5) resumes after fun2(3) returns: print 5
Backward output: 2 3 5
steps.note: Each function prints the same n it printed going down, creating a mirrored sequence.

steps.title: Full output
steps.note: The output is a palindrome around the innermost active call (fun1(2) printed 2 going in and 2 going out).

solution.result: The output printed when fun1(5) is called is 5 3 2 2 3 5 — option A.

questions.formula_note: Mutual recursion with symmetric print-before and print-after pattern produces a palindromic output; the forward half mirrors the backward half around the base case.
